From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American special effects artist (1946–2008)

| Stan Winston | |
| --- | --- |
| Winston at the [1997 Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/1997_Cannes_Film_Festival "1997 Cannes Film Festival") | |
| Born | Stanley Winston  (1946-04-07)April 7, 1946  [Richmond, Virginia](/wiki/Richmond,_Virginia "Richmond, Virginia"), U.S. |
| Died | June 15, 2008(2008-06-15) (aged 62)  [Malibu, California](/wiki/Malibu,_California "Malibu, California"), U.S. |
| Resting place | [Hillside Memorial Park Cemetery](/wiki/Hillside_Memorial_Park_Cemetery "Hillside Memorial Park Cemetery") |
| Occupation | Special effects artist |

**Stanley Winston**[[1]](#cite_note-1) (April 7, 1946 – June 15, 2008) was an American television and film [special make-up effects](/wiki/Prosthetic_makeup "Prosthetic makeup") artist, best known for his work in the [*Terminator* series](/wiki/Terminator_(franchise) "Terminator (franchise)"), the first three *[Jurassic Park](/wiki/Jurassic_Park_(franchise) "Jurassic Park (franchise)")* films, *[Aliens](/wiki/Aliens_(film) "Aliens (film)")*, *[The Thing](/wiki/The_Thing_(1982_film) "The Thing (1982 film)")*, the first two *[Predator](/wiki/Predator_(franchise) "Predator (franchise)")* films, *[Inspector Gadget](/wiki/Inspector_Gadget_(film) "Inspector Gadget (film)")*, *[Iron Man](/wiki/Iron_Man_(2008_film) "Iron Man (2008 film)")*, and *[Edward Scissorhands](/wiki/Edward_Scissorhands "Edward Scissorhands")*.[[2]](#cite_note-Cohen-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Crabtree-3)[[4]](#cite_note-SWS-4) He won four [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") for his work.

Winston, a frequent collaborator with directors [James Cameron](/wiki/James_Cameron "James Cameron"), [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg") and [Tim Burton](/wiki/Tim_Burton "Tim Burton"), owned several effects studios, including Stan Winston Digital. The established areas of expertise for Winston were in makeup, puppets and practical effects, but at the time of his death, he had expanded his studio to encompass digital effects as well.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stan_Winston&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Winston was born on April 7, 1946, in [Richmond, Virginia](/wiki/Richmond,_Virginia "Richmond, Virginia")[[5]](#cite_note-5) to a [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish "Jewish") family,[[6]](#cite_note-6) where he graduated from [Washington-Lee High School](/wiki/Washington-Lee_High_School "Washington-Lee High School") in 1964. He studied painting and sculpture at the [University of Virginia](/wiki/University_of_Virginia "University of Virginia") in [Charlottesville](/wiki/Charlottesville,_Virginia "Charlottesville, Virginia"), from which he graduated in 1968.[[4]](#cite_note-SWS-4)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stan_Winston&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

In 1969, after attending [California State University, Long Beach](/wiki/California_State_University,_Long_Beach "California State University, Long Beach"), Winston moved to [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles") to pursue a career as an actor. Struggling to find an acting job, he began a makeup apprenticeship at [Walt Disney Studios](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Pictures "Walt Disney Pictures").[[4]](#cite_note-SWS-4)

### 1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stan_Winston&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1970s")]

In 1972, Winston established his own company, Stan Winston Studio, and in 1973, he won an [Emmy Award](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award") for his effects work on the 1972 telefilm *[Gargoyles](/wiki/Gargoyles_(TV_film) "Gargoyles (TV film)")*. Over the next seven years, Winston continued to receive [Emmy Award](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award") nominations for work on projects and won another for 1974's *[The Autobiography of Miss Jane Pittman](/wiki/The_Autobiography_of_Miss_Jane_Pittman_(film) "The Autobiography of Miss Jane Pittman (film)")*. Winston also created the [Wookiee](/wiki/Wookiee "Wookiee") costumes for the 1978 *[Star Wars Holiday Special](/wiki/Star_Wars_Holiday_Special "Star Wars Holiday Special")*. In 1978, Winston was the Special Make-up Designer for *[The Wiz](/wiki/The_Wiz_(film) "The Wiz (film)")*.

### 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stan_Winston&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1980s")]

In 1982, Winston received his first Oscar nomination for *[Heartbeeps](/wiki/Heartbeeps "Heartbeeps")*, by which time he had set up his own studio. However, it was his ground-breaking work with [Rob Bottin](/wiki/Rob_Bottin "Rob Bottin") on his update of the science fiction horror classic *[The Thing](/wiki/The_Thing_(1982_film) "The Thing (1982 film)")* that year that brought him to prominence in Hollywood. He also worked on supervised vision work on *[The Entity](/wiki/The_Entity "The Entity")*. Between then, he contributed some special effects to *[Friday the 13th Part III](/wiki/Friday_the_13th_Part_III "Friday the 13th Part III")*, in which he made a slightly different head sculpt of [Jason](/wiki/Jason_Voorhees "Jason Voorhees") in an unused ending.

In 1983, Winston designed the [Mr. Roboto](/wiki/Mr._Roboto "Mr. Roboto") facemask for the American rock group [Styx](/wiki/Styx_(band) "Styx (band)").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In 1983 he also worked on the short-lived television series [Manimal](/wiki/Manimal "Manimal"), for which he created the panther and hawk transformation effects.

Winston reached a new level of fame in 1984 when [James Cameron](/wiki/James_Cameron "James Cameron")'s *[The Terminator](/wiki/The_Terminator "The Terminator")* premiered. The movie was a surprise hit, and Winston's work in bringing the titular metallic killing machine to life led to many new projects and additional collaborations with Cameron. In fact, Winston won his first [Academy Award for Best Visual Effects](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Visual_Effects "Academy Award for Best Visual Effects") in 1986 on James Cameron's next movie, *[Aliens](/wiki/Aliens_(film) "Aliens (film)")*.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Over the next few years, Winston and his company received more accolades for its work on many more Hollywood films, including [Tim Burton](/wiki/Tim_Burton "Tim Burton")'s *[Edward Scissorhands](/wiki/Edward_Scissorhands "Edward Scissorhands")*, [John McTiernan](/wiki/John_McTiernan "John McTiernan")'s *[Predator](/wiki/Predator_(film) "Predator (film)")*, *[Alien Nation](/wiki/Alien_Nation_(film) "Alien Nation (film)")*, *[The Monster Squad](/wiki/The_Monster_Squad "The Monster Squad")*, and *[Predator 2](/wiki/Predator_2 "Predator 2")*.

In 1988, Winston made his directorial debut with the horror movie *[Pumpkinhead](/wiki/Pumpkinhead_(film) "Pumpkinhead (film)")*, and won Best First Time Director at the Paris Film Festival. His next directing project was the child-friendly *[A Gnome Named Gnorm](/wiki/A_Gnome_Named_Gnorm "A Gnome Named Gnorm")* (1990), starring [Anthony Michael Hall](/wiki/Anthony_Michael_Hall "Anthony Michael Hall").

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stan_Winston&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1990s")]

James Cameron drafted Winston and his team once again in 1990, this time for *[Terminator 2: Judgment Day](/wiki/Terminator_2:_Judgment_Day "Terminator 2: Judgment Day")*. *T2* premiered in the summer of 1991, and Winston's work on this box office hit won him two more [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") for [Best Makeup](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Makeup_and_Hairstyling "Academy Award for Best Makeup and Hairstyling") and [Best Visual Effects](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Visual_Effects "Academy Award for Best Visual Effects").

In 1992, he was nominated for another [Tim Burton](/wiki/Tim_Burton "Tim Burton") film, the [superhero](/wiki/Superhero "Superhero") sequel *[Batman Returns](/wiki/Batman_Returns "Batman Returns")*, where he designed the makeup prosthetics for [Danny DeVito](/wiki/Danny_DeVito "Danny DeVito")'s [Penguin](/wiki/The_Penguin "The Penguin"). Additionally, his studio was commissioned to create robotic penguin puppets that were used throughout the film.

Winston turned his attention from super villains and [cyborgs](/wiki/Cyborg "Cyborg") to [dinosaurs](/wiki/Dinosaur "Dinosaur") when [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg") enlisted his help to bring [Michael Crichton](/wiki/Michael_Crichton "Michael Crichton")'s *[Jurassic Park](/wiki/Jurassic_Park_(novel) "Jurassic Park (novel)")* [to the cinema screen](/wiki/Jurassic_Park_(film) "Jurassic Park (film)"). In 1993, the movie became a blockbuster and Winston won another Oscar for [Best Visual Effects](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Visual_Effects "Academy Award for Best Visual Effects").

In 1993, Winston, Cameron and ex-[ILM](/wiki/Industrial_Light_%26_Magic "Industrial Light & Magic") General Manager [Scott Ross](/wiki/Scott_Ross_(film_executive) "Scott Ross (film executive)") co-founded [Digital Domain](/wiki/Digital_Domain "Digital Domain"), one of the foremost digital and visual effects studios in the world. In 1998, after the box office success of *[Titanic](/wiki/Titanic_(1997_film) "Titanic (1997 film)")*, Cameron and Winston severed their working relationship with the company and resigned from its board of directors.

Winston and his team continued to provide effects work for many more films and expanded their work into [animatronics](/wiki/Animatronics "Animatronics"). Some of Winston's notable animatronics work can be found in *[The Ghost and the Darkness](/wiki/The_Ghost_and_the_Darkness "The Ghost and the Darkness")* and *[T2-3D: Battle Across Time](/wiki/T2-3D:_Battle_Across_Time "T2-3D: Battle Across Time")*, James Cameron's 3-D continuation of the *Terminator* series for the [Universal Studios theme parks](/wiki/Universal_Destinations_%26_Experiences "Universal Destinations & Experiences"). One of Winston's most ambitious animatronics projects was Steven Spielberg's *[A.I. Artificial Intelligence](/wiki/A.I._Artificial_Intelligence "A.I. Artificial Intelligence")*, which earned Winston another Oscar nomination for [Best Visual Effects](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Visual_Effects "Academy Award for Best Visual Effects").

In 1996, Winston directed and co-produced the longest music video of all time, *[Ghosts](/wiki/Michael_Jackson%27s_Ghosts "Michael Jackson's Ghosts")*, which was based on an original concept of [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson") and [Stephen King](/wiki/Stephen_King "Stephen King"). The long-form music video presented a number of never before seen visual effects, and promoted music from *[Blood on the Dance Floor: HIStory in the Mix](/wiki/Blood_on_the_Dance_Floor:_HIStory_in_the_Mix "Blood on the Dance Floor: HIStory in the Mix")*, which went on to become the biggest selling remix album of all time (13 million).

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stan_Winston&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2000s")]

In 2001, Winston, together with [Colleen Camp](/wiki/Colleen_Camp "Colleen Camp") and [Samuel Z. Arkoff](/wiki/Samuel_Z._Arkoff "Samuel Z. Arkoff")'s son, [Lou Arkoff](/wiki/Lou_Arkoff "Lou Arkoff"), produced a series of made-for-cable films for [Cinemax](/wiki/Cinemax "Cinemax") and [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO"). The five films, referred to as *[Creature Features](/wiki/Creature_Features#2001,_Cinemax "Creature Features")*, were inspired by the titles of [AIP](/wiki/American_International_Pictures "American International Pictures") monster movies from the 1950s — i.e., *[Earth vs. the Spider](/wiki/Earth_vs._the_Spider "Earth vs. the Spider")* (1958), *[How to Make a Monster](/wiki/How_to_Make_a_Monster_(1958_film) "How to Make a Monster (1958 film)")* (1958), *[Day the World Ended](/wiki/Day_the_World_Ended "Day the World Ended")* (1955), *[The She-Creature](/wiki/The_She_Creature "The She Creature")* (1956), and *[Teenage Caveman](/wiki/Teenage_Caveman_(1958_film) "Teenage Caveman (1958 film)")* (1958) — but had completely different plots.[[9]](#cite_note-9) two reasons Stan Winston did this was because he'd had worked with AIP in their last years providing special effects for *[The Bat People](/wiki/The_Bat_People "The Bat People")* (1974) and start a toy line with action figures from the aforementioned four film remakes.[[10]](#cite_note-Cinefan-Interview-10)

In 2002 Winston helped to launch a new comic line, Stan Winston's Realm Of The Claw / Mutant Earth which was a flip book and ran for 4 issues. Stan Winston's Trakk Monster Hunter came out in 2003 and ran for 2 issues. These were published by Image Comics.

In 2003, Winston was invited by the [Smithsonian Institution](/wiki/Smithsonian_Institution "Smithsonian Institution") to speak about his life and career in a public presentation sponsored by The Lemelson Center for the Study of Invention and Innovation. The presentation took place on November 15, 2003, at the Smithsonian's [National Museum of American History](/wiki/National_Museum_of_American_History "National Museum of American History").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Winston also worked on *[Terminator 3: Rise of the Machines](/wiki/Terminator_3:_Rise_of_the_Machines "Terminator 3: Rise of the Machines")*.

By April 2003, Winston was working on his next project, *Jurassic Park IV*.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
By April 2005, Winston said the film was on hold.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The film would eventually be released in 2015 titled *[Jurassic World](/wiki/Jurassic_World "Jurassic World")*.

At the time of his death, Winston was working on the fourth *Terminator* film, *[Terminator Salvation](/wiki/Terminator_Salvation "Terminator Salvation")*,[[14]](#cite_note-14) as well as frequent collaborator James Cameron's film *[Avatar](/wiki/Avatar_(2009_film) "Avatar (2009 film)")*. Winston designed the original monsters that appeared in the [Midway](/wiki/Midway_Games "Midway Games") game *[The Suffering](/wiki/The_Suffering_(video_game) "The Suffering (video game)")*[[15]](#cite_note-15) and its sequel, *[The Suffering: Ties That Bind](/wiki/The_Suffering:_Ties_That_Bind "The Suffering: Ties That Bind")*.

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stan_Winston&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Death")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Traceroute--Johannes_Grenzfurthner_and_Matt_Winston.jpg/250px-Traceroute--Johannes_Grenzfurthner_and_Matt_Winston.jpg)](/wiki/File:Traceroute--Johannes_Grenzfurthner_and_Matt_Winston.jpg)

[Johannes Grenzfurthner](/wiki/Johannes_Grenzfurthner "Johannes Grenzfurthner") and [Matt Winston](/wiki/Matt_Winston "Matt Winston") talk about Stan Winston and special effects in the 2016 documentary *[Traceroute](/wiki/Traceroute_(film) "Traceroute (film)")*

Stan Winston died on June 15, 2008, in [Malibu, California](/wiki/Malibu,_California "Malibu, California"), after suffering for seven years from [multiple myeloma](/wiki/Multiple_myeloma "Multiple myeloma").[[2]](#cite_note-Cohen-2) A spokeswoman reported that he "died peacefully at home surrounded by family."[[3]](#cite_note-Crabtree-3) Winston was with his wife and two children, actor [Matt Winston](/wiki/Matt_Winston "Matt Winston") and Debbie Winston. [Arnold Schwarzenegger](/wiki/Arnold_Schwarzenegger "Arnold Schwarzenegger") made a public speech about his death, and [Jon Favreau](/wiki/Jon_Favreau "Jon Favreau") dedicated his Spike TV [Scream Award](/wiki/Scream_Awards "Scream Awards") to him upon receiving the award for Best Sci-Fi Movie for *[Iron Man](/wiki/Iron_Man_(2008_film) "Iron Man (2008 film)")*. *[Terminator Salvation](/wiki/Terminator_Salvation "Terminator Salvation")* is dedicated to both Winston and Joseph R. Kubicek Sr, with the dedication appearing at both the beginning and end of the film. After his death, his four supervisors (Shane Mahan, John Rosengrant, Alan Scott, Lindsay Macgowan) founded and built their own studio, [Legacy Effects](/wiki/Legacy_Effects "Legacy Effects"), named to honor his memory.[[16]](#cite_note-16) A private funeral was held for Winston at Hillside Memorial Park.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Stan Winston School

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stan_Winston&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Stan Winston School")]

In 2009, the year after his death, the Winston family founded the [Stan Winston School of Character Arts](https://www.stanwinstonschool.com/) to "preserve Stan's legacy by inspiring and fostering creativity in a new generation of character creators.”[[18]](#cite_note-18) The school, which is 100% online, currently offers hundreds of in-depth, on-demand educational video courses by Hollywood's leading special effects artists and creators. Topics covered include design, sculpture, mold making, lab work, animatronics, makeup effects, puppet making, painting, and fabrication.
